content.full_text:
Farn Polrrrcer PnacrrcEs CouvussroN
428 J Street o Suite 620 o Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 . Fax (916) 322-0886
May 4,20M
Louis Blumberg
5285 Diamond Heights Blvd., #306
SanFrancisco;CA 94131
Ygur. Request for Advice
Our File No. A-03-295'
Dear Mr. Blumberg:
This letter is in response to your request for advice regarding the post-
govefllmental employment provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act').I The
conclusions in this letter are based on the information you have provided. The Fair
Political Practices Commission ("Commission') does not act as a finder of fact when
providing advice. (In re Oglesby (197 5) I FPPC Ops. 7l ; section 33 I 14.) In addition,
the Commission does not provide advice relating to past conduct. (Regulation
1832e(bX8XA).)
QUESTTON
As the former deputy director for communications and legislation at the
Deparfrnent of Forestry and Fire Protection, does the one-year ban apply to your
appearances before the Board of Forestry and Fire Protection?
CONCLUSION
Yes. Because the Board of Forestry and Fire Protection is under the direction and
control of the Department of Forestry and Fire Protection for purposes of the revolving
door provisions of the Act (see analysis), you are precluded from appearing before the
Board for a period of one year after leaving state service. In addition, you must also
comply with the provisions of the Act's permanent ban.
I Govemment Code sections 81000 - g1}l4. Commission regulations appear at Title 2, sections
18109-18997, of the Califomia Code of Regulations.
Re


File No. A-03-295
Page No. 2
FACTS
The following is based on coffespondence and phone conversations with you and
George Genky, Executive Director of the Board of Forestry and Fire Protection.
Governor Davis appointed you as the deputy director for communications and legislation
at the Deparknent of Forestry and Fire Protection ('Deparhnent') in October 1999. You
were then appointed as the deputy director for communications at the Departrnent of
Industrial Relations by Governor Davis in August 2003. You left state service in January
2004 to work for the Nature Conservancy, where your duties are policy-related. You
seek adviceregardinghow section87406 (one-yearban) applies to yourappearances
before the Board of Forestuy and Fire Protection ("Board').
As deputy director for communications and legislation at the Deparhnent, you
were the lead public affairs representative for the Departrnent and, as such, were in
charge of communications, mediq and public education. In addition, you were part of
the liaison team which worked with the Governor's office on legislation. You state that
you had no interactions with the Board except that you may have sent news clippings to
that agency when they were mentioned in an article.
The Department and the Board of Foresky and Fire Protection have separate staf{
but both entities operate under the same budget, are housed in the same building, and are
governed by the same conflict of interest codes. You mention a Mendocino County
Superior Court decision in which the judge.ruled that theDeparfinent and the Board were
separate and distinct for "lead agency stafus" pursuant to the California Environmental
Quality Act (CEQA). ln addition, you provided sections of the Fublic Resources Code
and Title 14 of the California Code of Regulations which establish an appeal process for
timber harvesting plans denied by the director. Pursuant to the regulations, the Board
acts as an independent review agency for purposes of hearing and resolving appeals from
the public.
According to Mr. Gentry, the Board governs the Deparhnent as to policy, in that
the Board promulgates policy which the Deparfinent.implements. In addition, while in
many instances the Board feels as apracticalmatter that they are separate from the
Department, Public Resources Code section 730(a), the statute establishing the Board's
existence, states, "[t]here is in the department a State Board of Forestry and Fire
Protection. . . ." (Section 730(a).)
ANALYSIS
The Act has two main post-governmental employment restrictions on individuals
who have left state service:
1) A "one-yearban" prohibiting a state employee from communicating with his or
her former agency to influence the agency's administrative or legislative action (section
87 406, regulations I 87 46.1 and 187 46.2); and


File No. A-03-295
Page No. 3
2) A "permanent ban" barring a state employee from "switching sides" in any
specific proceeding in which the employee was involved while in state service (sections
87 400-87 405, regulation I 874 1. l).
Your letter concerns the post-employment issue colloquially known as the "one-
yearban." The Act places certain restrictions on individuals who have recently left state
service and who wish to use the expertise and the relationships they developed at their
former agency for compensation by third persons. (Sections 87400 - 87407.)
As the former deputy director for communications and legislation at the
Department of Forestry and Fire Protection, you are a state adminiskative official and
subject to the revolving door provisions of the Act. (Section S7400(b).)
One-Year Ban
Section 87406 of the Act prohibits specified officials, for one year after leaving
state service, from being paid to communicate with their former agency in an attempt to
influence agency decisions which involve the making of general rules, or to influence
certain proceedings involving a permit, license, conkact, or hansaction involving
property or goods. (See also regulations 18746.1 and 18746.2.)
Section 87406(d)(l) of the Act provides, in pertinent part, that no ofEcer or
designated employee of a state administrative age,ncy:
"[F]or a period of one year after leaving office or
employment, shall, for compensation, act as agent or
attomey for, or otherwise represent, any other person, by
making any formal or informal appearance, or by making
any oral or written communication, before any state
administrative agency, or officer or employee thereof, for
which he or she worked or represented during the 12
months before leaving office or employment, ifthe
appearance-or communication is made for the purpose of
influeneing2 administrative or legislative action,3 or
influencing any action or proceeding involving the
issuance, amendment, awarding, or revocation of a permit,
license, Brffit, or contract, or the sale or purchase of goods
2 "Influencing legislative or administative action" includes influencing by any means, "including
but not limited to the provision or use of information, statistics, studies or analyses." (Section 82032.)
"Administrative action" is defined in section 82002 as "the proposal, drafting, developmen! consideration,
amendment, enactment, or defeat by any state agency of any de, regulation, or other action in any rate-
making proceeding or any quasi-legislative proceeding. . .."
3 Section 82037 defines "legislativi action" is "the drafting, inkoduction, consideration,
modification, enactntent or defeat of any bill, resolution, amendment, report, nomination or other matter by
the Legislature or by either house or any committee, subcommittee, joint or select committee thereof, or by
a member or employee of the Legislature acting in his official capacity. 'Legislative action' also means the
action of the Govemor in approving or vetoing any bill."


FileNo. A-03-295
Page No. 4
or property. For purposes of this paragraph, an appearance
before a state administrative agency does not include an
appearance in a court of law, before an administrative law
judge, or before the Workers' Compensation Appeals
Board...."
Regulation 1 87 46.1 (copy enclosed) outlines when the one-year prohibition
applies. Section (bX6) of that regulation states that the appearance is prohibited ifl
"(6) The appearance or communication is madebefore any
ofEcer or employee of any of the following:
"(A) Any state administrative agency that the public
official worked for or represented during the 12 months
before leaving state office or employment. An employee
loaned to an agency is deemed to have worked for or
represented that agency.
"(B) Any state administrative agency which budget,
personnel, and other operations are subject to the direction
and control of any a5ency described in subdivision
OX6XA). However, whether an agency is provided
technical assistance or legal advice, or is subject to
oversight by another agency pursuant to state law, are not
factors to be considered in determining whether an agency
is subject to the direction and control of another.
"(C) Any state administrative agency subject to the
direction and control of the Governor, if the official was a
designated employee of the Governor's office during the 12
months before leaving state office or emplo5rment.,,
An appearance or communication is:
"...for the purpose of influencing if it is made for the
principal purpose of supporting, promoting influencing,
modifuing, opposing, delaying, or advancing the action or
proceeding. An appearance or communication includes,
but is not limited to, conversing by telephone or in person,
corresponding with in writing or by electronic kansmission,
attending a meeting, and delivering or sending any
communication." (Regulatio n 187 46.2(a).)
Determining *State Administrative Agency,,
According to regulation 18746.1(b)(6), the one-year ban provision of the Act
applies when the "appearance or communication is made before any officer or employee
of any of the following: (A) [a]ny state administrative agency that the public official
worked for or represented during the 12 months before leaving state office or


File No. A-03-295
Page No. 5
ernployment." In your case, you worked for the Deparfrrnent and the Deparhnent of
Industrial Relations, so the one-year ban applies to your appearance before the
Departrnent and the Deparfinent of Induskial Relations.
The one-year ban also applies to:
"Any state administrative agency which budget, personnel,
and other operations are subject to the direction and control
of any agency described in subdivision (bX6XA).
However, whether an agency is provided technical
assistance or legal advice, or is subject to oversight by
another agency pursuant to state law, are not factors to be
considered in determining whether an agency is subject to
the direction and control of another." (Regulation
18746.1(b)(6XB).)4
From the facts you have provided, although you maintain that the Board and
Department have separate staff the Departrnent controls the budget for both itself and the
Board.
Similarly, in the Corum letter, No. A-02-258, we advised that, "[s]ince the County
Property Tax Division of the BOE does not have its own budgetary authority, but is
subordinate to and under the control of the BOE [State Board of Equalization], your
former state administrative agency employer is the BOE, and the one-year ban extends
agency-wide across the BOE including regional offices." In your case as well, the Board
does not have its own budgetary authority separate from that of the Department.
In addition, the Board and D€,parhnent share the same conflict of interest code
governing their ernployees. We have previously analyzed, a similar situation pertaining to
the Board of Education and the Curriculum Commission. (Grffith Advice Letter, No. A-
02-334.) In the Grffith letter, where a former ernployee had worked for the Curriculum
Commission, we gave weight to the fact that both members of the Board of Education
and members of the Curriculum Commission were listed and assigned disclosure
categories within the Board's conflict of interest code. We concluded that since the
Board of Education's conflict of interest code characterizedmembers of the Commission
as employees of the Board, the requestor was prohibited from appearing before the
Curriculum Commission and the Board of Education forpurposes ofthe revolving door
provisions. As in Grffith, use of the same conflict of interest code by both the Board and
the Department, your former employer, indicates that personnel of these two entities have
the same or similar functions and are governed within one agency. Furthermore, it
a Please note that regulation 1 8746. 1(b) sets forth a six-step process for determining whether the
one-year ban applies. (Staff memorandum to commissioners entitled, "Adoption of Regulation
18746.1...and Regulation 18746.2...,"December22, 1998.) This regulation describes which agencies
subject a former employee to the one-year ban. The distinction you noted between the Departrnent and the
Board for puqposes of CEQA requirements is not deterrninative of whether the one-year ban will apply to a
particular agency. Instead, the focus is on whether one agency contols the budget, personnel, and
operations of the other.


File No. A-03-295
Page No. 6
appears that both agencies also share other operations in that they are housed in the same
building and ostensibly share the same faciliiies. Therefore, we conclude that the
provisions ofregulationlST46.l@X6xB) are met. consequently, you may not
communicate with or appear, as described above, before the Depariment oiForestry and
:11^lY::1i?n 
or the Board of Forestry and Fire Protection forlzmonths after teaving
state serylce."
For your information, we are enclosing a copy of the Commission,s fact sheet
entitted, "LeavingYour State Job? Post-Empl,oy-*i Restrictions Mayaffect you.,' lf
you have any other questions regarding this maiter, please contact me at (g16) 322-5660.
Sincerely,
Luisa Menchaca
General Counsel
By:t
J
t-,
Counsel,
Division
Enclosures
NB je
I:\Adviceltrs\03-295
5 This conclusion is consistent with both Public Resources Code section 730(a)and the Bischel
Advice r*tler, No. I-95-287, where a fonner executive officer for the Foresters ri"""rlrj committee of the
leplrtnent of Foresty was subject to the one-year ban. ln Bischet letter, we did not disiingulsh between
the Deparhnent and the Board as beirg separate agencies.

Re

Deparfrnent of Forestry and Fire Protection, does the one-year ban apply to your
appearances before the Board of Forestry and Fire Protection?

CONCLUSION: Yes. Because the Board of Forestry and Fire Protection is under the direction and
control of the Department of Forestry and Fire Protection for purposes of the revolving
door provisions of the Act (see analysis), you are precluded from appearing before the
Board for a period of one year after leaving state service. In addition, you must also
comply with the provisions of the Act's permanent ban.
Re
